sanskrit_devanagari:
अव्यक्तादीनि भूतानि व्यक्तमध्यानि भारत ।
अव्यक्तनिधनान्येव तत्र का परिदेवना ॥ २८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
avyaktādīni bhūtāni vyakta-madhyāni bhārata |
avyakta-nidhanāny eva tatra kā paridevanā || 28 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; line-break inserted between hemistichs in source pack)
exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.028 = MBh_06,024.028). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: All created beings are unmanifest in their beginning, manifest in their interim state, and unmanifest again when annihilated. So what need is there for lamentation?
O scion of Bharat, all created beings are unmanifest before birth, manifest in life, and again unmanifest on death. So why grieve?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Accepting that there are two classes of philosophers, one believing in the existence of the soul and the other not believing in the existence of the soul, there is no cause for lamentation in either case. Nonbelievers in the existence of the soul are called atheists by followers of Vedic wisdom.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.28 It is not reasonable to grieve even for beings which are constituted by bodies and organs, since 'all beings remain unmanifest' etc. Bhutani, all beings, avyaktādini, remain unmanifest in the beginning. Those beings, viz sons, friends, and others, who before their origination have unmanifestedness.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as a structural argument: every composite being has the same three-phase shape — unmanifest before, manifest during, unmanifest after. The middle phase, in which the being is visible, is bracketed on both sides by non-being. Loved ones (sons, friends) were unmanifest to you before birth and will be unmanifest to you after death. Mourning the bracket as though it were the whole structure is a category error — you cannot mourn a thing for resuming the state it was in before you knew it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.28 Human beings etc., (i.e., bodies) exist as entities; their previous stages are unknown, their middle stages in the form of man etc., are known, and their (final) and future stages are unknown. As they thus exist in their own natural stages, there is no cause for grief.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja restates the structure in epistemic terms: the being's previous stages are unknown to us, the middle stage is known, the final stages are unknown. The known middle is the smallest of the three phases. Treating the known phase as the privileged one — the only one that 'really' exists — is a perceptual artifact, not a metaphysical fact. The verse extends 2.26-2.27's concession into a third independent argument: even on bodily-existence grounds, the three-phase structure of being makes mourning incoherent.

positions.rendering: unmanifest in their beginning... unmanifest again when annihilated
unmanifest before birth... unmanifest on death
unmanifest before origination — refers specifically to composite beings (sons, friends) seen by another
previous stages unknown, future stages unknown — epistemic framing of the bracket

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translations agree on the literal three-phase structure (unmanifest → manifest → unmanifest). The traditional commentaries diverge on whether the verse applies to (a) composite beings as known to a perceiver — Shankara emphasises sons and friends, beings whose existence is known relationally — or (b) the metaphysical structure of all manifested existence in itself. Ramanuja's epistemic reframing ('unknown stages bracket the known stage') is the strongest reading: the verse is honest about the perceptual asymmetry between the bracket and the middle. Both readings reach the same conclusion: paridevanā (lamentation) is incoherent given the structure.

literal_meaning: All beings are unmanifest in their beginning, manifest in the middle, and unmanifest again at the end, O Bhārata. What lamentation is there in this?
traditional_meaning_consensus: The third independent argument against grief, complementing 2.26-2.27. Every manifested being has a three-phase structure: unmanifest pre-existence, manifest middle, unmanifest post-existence. The middle phase is the smallest of the three; mourning the loss of the middle phase is mourning a thing for resuming the state it was in before. The verse is independent of the imperishable-Self doctrine — it works at the level of phenomenal beings (bhūtāni). The commentarial tradition (Shankara, Ramanuja, Sridhara) reads this as the verse that closes off even the perceptual escape route — even from the standpoint of what you can see, mourning fails. The triplet 2.26-2.27-2.28 forms a closed argument: hypothetically (2.26), structurally (2.27), perceptually (2.28), grief is incoherent.
